jokes.text: Uh, excuse me. I missed a few lectures. Uh, what subject is this? Ancient Egyptian Algebra. What a nightmare!
jokes.setup: Fry asks what subject the final exam is for
jokes.punchline: It's Ancient Egyptian Algebra and his immediate 'What a nightmare!' reaction
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Fry's fish-out-of-water status. The specificity of 'Ancient Egyptian Algebra' adds absurdist flavor.

jokes.text: Mr. Fry, are those your underpants?
jokes.setup: Fry is unprepared for an exam
jokes.punchline: The professor notices he's wearing underwear outside his clothes or in some inappropriate way
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag that plays into the dream logic. Economy is good but fairly predictable embarrassment humor.

jokes.text: Young man, I think it's time you learned a lesson... about Lightspeed brand briefs.
jokes.setup: Professor threatens to teach Fry a lesson
jokes.punchline: It's actually a commercial advertisement embedded in his dream
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that introduces the episode's concept of dream advertising. The seamless transition from discipline to commercial is clever writing.

jokes.text: Style and comfort for the discriminating crotch.
jokes.setup: Generic underwear commercial copy
jokes.punchline: 'Discriminating crotch' as upscale marketing speak
jokes.explanation: Funny phrase that parodies advertising language. 'Discriminating crotch' is memorable and quotable.

jokes.text: The ad gets into your brain just like this liquid gets into this egg. Although in reality it's not liquid, but gamma radiation.
jokes.setup: Professor demonstrates how dream ads work with seemingly innocent egg demonstration
jokes.punchline: Casually reveals it's actually gamma radiation
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with academic matter-of-factness. The casual reveal of radiation poisoning is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Well, sure, but not in our dreams only on TV and radio and in magazines and movies and at ball games and on buses and milk cartons and T-shirts and bananas and written on the sky but not in dreams. No, sirree.
jokes.setup: Fry protests that the 20th century didn't have intrusive advertising
jokes.punchline: Lists an absurdly long catalog of where ads actually were, undermining his own point
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalating list that builds momentum while completely undermining Fry's premise. Perfect character writing - Fry's obliviousness to his own contradiction.

jokes.text: Nobody's forcing you to buy anything. Yeah. I mean, we all had commercials in our dreams but you don't see us running off to buy brand-name merchandise at low, low prices.
jokes.setup: Bender claims dream ads don't influence people
jokes.punchline: Immediately uses advertising language while making his point
jokes.explanation: Nice ironic moment where Bender unknowingly proves the opposite of his point. Good character consistency for Bender's self-awareness gaps.

jokes.text: What a lovely face. We just need to draw attention away from the eye area.
jokes.setup: Salesperson compliments Leela
jokes.punchline: Immediately suggests hiding her most distinctive feature (single eye)
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor targeting Leela's cyclops nature. The false compliment followed by brutal honesty works well.

jokes.text: Can I try these on before I buy them? I'm afraid I can't let you open the package but you can try on the demo pair.
jokes.setup: Fry wants to try on sunglasses before buying
jokes.punchline: The demo pair policy for something as personal as eyewear
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor about retail policies. Functional but not particularly inspired.

jokes.text: Ooh. Ho-ho-ho.
jokes.setup: Fry tries on the demo sunglasses
jokes.punchline: His pleased reaction suggests they look ridiculous on him
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag. Fry's satisfaction with probably ridiculous-looking sunglasses fits his character but isn't particularly clever.

jokes.text: Hey, Bender. Great new sweater. Uh, 'new'? What sweater? I came in with it! I don't know you people.
jokes.setup: Fry compliments Bender's sweater
jokes.punchline: Bender obviously just shoplifted it but denies knowing them
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender characterization - caught red-handed shoplifting and immediately denying association. The escalation to 'I don't know you people' is great.

jokes.text: Visa hasn't existed for 500 years. American Express? 600 years. Discover Card? Mmm, sorry, we don't take Discover.
jokes.setup: Fry tries various credit cards from his era
jokes.punchline: The punchline that even in the future, nobody takes Discover Card
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating gag that builds to a perfect observational punchline about Discover Card's perpetual acceptance issues. Great structure and timing.

jokes.text: What kind of world is this where they advertise things not everybody can afford?
jokes.setup: Fry can't afford the underwear
jokes.punchline: His naive question about advertising unaffordable things
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Fry's fish-out-of-water perspective. The irony of his complaint about capitalism works well.

jokes.text: Hey, who's the rocker jockey? Guh! It's Mom the world's most huggable industrialist.
jokes.setup: Fry doesn't recognize Mom on TV
jokes.punchline: Leela's description of her as 'most huggable industrialist' - oxymoronic corporate PR
jokes.explanation: 'Most huggable industrialist' is a funny oxymoron that establishes Mom's false public persona. Good world-building humor.

jokes.text: 'Mom,' 'Love' and 'Screen Door' are registered trademarks of MomCorp.
jokes.setup: Mom's robot oil commercial with folksy language
jokes.punchline: Even common words like 'Mom' and 'Love' are trademarked
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of corporate overreach. The idea of trademarking basic emotional concepts is both absurd and uncomfortably plausible.

jokes.text: Hamboned.
jokes.setup: Bender is about to be arrested
jokes.punchline: His nonsensical exclamation 'Hamboned'
jokes.explanation: Typical Bender non-sequitur. 'Hamboned' as an expression of frustration is random but fits his character's weird verbal tics.

jokes.text: I say, I'm sure there's... That is, I'm sure there's... a very... reasonable...
jokes.setup: Bender tries to talk his way out of arrest
jokes.punchline: Each attempt to speak gets more pathetic as he's presumably being beaten
jokes.explanation: Good escalating physical comedy implied through dialogue degradation. Bender's failed attempts at dignified speech work well.

jokes.text: I'd love to chip in, but Bender stole my wallet.
jokes.setup: They need money for Bender's bail
jokes.punchline: Fry can't contribute because Bender robbed him
jokes.explanation: Good ironic situation - victim can't help bail out their victimizer. Perfectly captures Bender's relationship with the crew.

jokes.text: Sure. It's the price of a cheese pizza and a large soda back where I used to work Pinucci's Pizza.
jokes.setup: Bank asks for Fry's PIN number
jokes.punchline: It's memorably tied to pizza prices from his old job
jokes.explanation: Great character detail that feels authentic. Using pizza prices as a PIN is both relatable and sets up the later plot point perfectly.

jokes.text: At an average of 2¼ percent interest over a period of 1,000 years that comes to... $4.3 billion.
jokes.setup: Calculating compound interest on 93 cents
jokes.punchline: It becomes $4.3 billion after 1,000 years
jokes.explanation: Brilliant mathematical sight gag that drives the entire episode plot. The absurd transformation from 93 cents to billions is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: Maybe you don't understand just how rich he is. In fact, I think I better put on a monocle.
jokes.setup: Bender questions wearing top hats for rich Fry
jokes.punchline: Leela decides she needs an even more pretentious rich person accessory
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the rich person stereotype accessories. Monocle as the next level up from top hat is well-chosen.

jokes.text: Anchovies. You know those little headless fish? Does not compute.
jokes.setup: Fry orders anchovies and explains what they are
jokes.punchline: The pizza guy's computer-like response of confusion
jokes.explanation: Sets up the episode's central MacGuffin. The robot pizza guy's literal 'does not compute' response works for the world-building.

jokes.text: Just about the time your people arrived on earth wasn't it, Zoidberg? I'm not on trial here.
jokes.setup: Discussing when anchovies went extinct
jokes.punchline: Zoidberg's defensive response suggests his people's guilt
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Zoidberg. His immediate defensiveness implies guilt while maintaining plausible deniability.

jokes.text: Stop! Stop! I admit it. My people ate them all. We kept saying, 'One more can't hurt.' And then they were gone. We're sorry!
jokes.setup: Fry describing how great anchovies were
jokes.punchline: Zoidberg breaks down and confesses to ecological genocide
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from Zoidberg's denial to full confession. The 'one more can't hurt' logic perfectly captures how environmental destruction happens.

jokes.text: Well, aside from causing eye cancer these things had a lousy, low-definition picture.
jokes.setup: Commenting on Fry's primitive 20th century TV
jokes.punchline: Eye cancer mentioned casually before complaining about picture quality
jokes.explanation: Great priorities joke - mentioning cancer casually then focusing on picture quality. Good deadpan delivery structure.

jokes.text: On a TV like this, I bet you couldn't even make out my obscene tattoo.
jokes.setup: Discussing low resolution of old TVs
jokes.punchline: Bender apparently has an obscene tattoo that would be visible on better TVs
jokes.explanation: Good character detail for Bender. The idea of a robot having an obscene tattoo fits his personality perfectly.

jokes.text: Who was this Ted Danson and why would you pay $10,000 for his skeleton?
jokes.setup: Questioning Fry's auction purchase
jokes.punchline: Fry bought Ted Danson's skeleton for $10,000
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd and dark. The casual mention of buying a celebrity skeleton is perfectly bizarre for Futurama's world.

jokes.text: I have an idea for a sitcom.
jokes.setup: Looking at Ted Danson's skeleton
jokes.punchline: Fry thinks he can create a sitcom from bones
jokes.explanation: Meta humor about TV creation. Fry's naive optimism about making entertainment from celebrity remains is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Don't tell me how to spend my money. 50, going once, twice... $75,000. Oh, my God. It's Mom.
jokes.setup: Fry bidding aggressively on anchovies
jokes.punchline: Mom enters the bidding war, shocking everyone
jokes.explanation: Good plot escalation. Mom's entrance raises the stakes and sets up the main conflict. The shock reaction works well.

jokes.text: One jillion dollars. Sir, that's not a number. Oh. In that case 50 million.
jokes.setup: Fry makes an impossible bid
jokes.punchline: He doesn't know 'jillion' isn't real, then settles for $50 million like it's nothing
jokes.explanation: Great character comedy showing Fry's mathematical ignorance and casual attitude toward massive amounts of money. Well-structured with good timing.

jokes.text: Do you remember a time when chocolate chip cookies came fresh from the oven? Petridge Farm remembers. Do you remember a time when women couldn't vote and certain folk weren't allowed on golf courses? Petridge Farm remembers.
jokes.setup: Nostalgic Pepperidge Farm parody starts with innocent cookies
jokes.punchline: Escalates to remembering terrible historical discrimination
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation parody of Pepperidge Farm commercials. The progression from cookies to civil rights violations is perfectly dark and unexpected. This became iconic.

jokes.text: I like big butts and I cannot lie # You other brothers can't deny
jokes.setup: Fry claims to listen to 'classical music'
jokes.punchline: It's Sir Mix-a-Lot's 'Baby Got Back'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Fry's lowbrow taste being 'classical' to future

jokes.text: I could if you hadn't turned on the lights and shut off the stereo.
jokes.setup: Leela says he can't sit in the dark listening to classical music
jokes.punchline: Fry's literal-minded response about the technical impediments
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan response showing Fry's literal thinking. His focus on the technical rather than emotional issues is in character.

jokes.text: Now, are you going to come to the squid fights with us or sit here wallowing in your prehistoric junk?
jokes.setup: Trying to get Fry to socialize
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of 'squid fights' as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Good world-building humor. 'Squid fights' casually mentioned as normal 3000s entertainment adds to the alien future setting.

jokes.text: I finally found what I need to be happy and it's not friends... it's things. I'm a thing.
jokes.setup: Fry declares he doesn't need friends, only things
jokes.punchline: Bender's matter-of-fact self-identification as a thing
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment. Bender's deadpan response perfectly captures his robot nature and desire to be included, even as an object.

jokes.text: My ponytail's caught in the door.
jokes.setup: Leela trying to storm out dramatically
jokes.punchline: Her dramatic exit is ruined by getting physically stuck
jokes.explanation: Classic physical comedy undermining a dramatic moment. The ponytail caught in the door deflates the tension effectively.

jokes.text: Holy crap, that bastard's itchy.
jokes.setup: Mom removing her sweet public persona
jokes.punchline: Crude complaint about her disguise being uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: Great character revelation showing Mom's true crude nature. The contrast with her public sweet persona is well-executed.

jokes.text: Walt, cream soda. Right away, Mother. Larry, get your mother a cream soda. But Mom said... You heard me.
jokes.setup: Mom asks Walt for cream soda, he immediately delegates to Larry
jokes.punchline: Creates pointless bureaucracy for a simple request
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing the sons' incompetent corporate hierarchy. The immediate delegation creates absurd inefficiency.

jokes.text: You don't have to do the math to know that's a buttload of oil.
jokes.setup: Mom explaining her robot oil business calculations
jokes.punchline: Uses 'buttload' as a technical measurement unit
jokes.explanation: Mom's crude language contrasting with business presentation. 'Buttload' as a unit of measurement fits her character's vulgarity.

jokes.text: If anyone ever got hold of anchovy DNA they could chop out the oil-making gene stick it in a bunch of third world kids and bam! Cheap, effective robot oil
jokes.setup: Explaining the anchovy threat to her business
jokes.punchline: Casually suggests horrific genetic experimentation on children
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark humor showing Mom's evil nature. The casual suggestion of child exploitation reveals her true character perfectly.

jokes.text: Esther, you ugly.
jokes.setup: Mom looking in mirror
jokes.punchline: Insults herself in third person with poor grammar
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Mom's self-loathing. The grammatically incorrect self-insult feels authentic to her crude nature.

jokes.text: Mother, you are one clever old scag. And don't you forget it.
jokes.setup: Walt compliments his mother's plan
jokes.punchline: Calls her a 'scag' as a compliment, which she accepts
jokes.explanation: Good family dynamic humor. The fact that 'clever old scag' is meant and received as a compliment shows their dysfunctional relationship.

jokes.text: Now I'm off to some charity B.S. For knocked-up teenage sluts.
jokes.setup: Mom leaving to attend a charity event
jokes.punchline: Her crude description of what should be a compassionate cause
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Mom's public facade vs. private vulgarity. Her attitude toward charity work reveals her true nature.

jokes.text: We're here to tighten your drains. Ow!
jokes.setup: Fake plumbers at Fry's door
jokes.punchline: 'Tighten your drains' makes no sense, followed by immediate violence
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that doesn't make plumbing sense, followed by slapstick. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: No, you only 'dreamed' you were in the year 3000.
jokes.setup: Fry questioning if he's really back in 2000
jokes.punchline: The obvious lie with suspicious air quotes around 'dreamed'
jokes.explanation: The air quotes make the deception obvious to the audience. Meta humor about the show's premise but not particularly sophisticated.

jokes.text: Okay, but I'm only doing this so people will take my head seriously as an actress.
jokes.setup: Pamela Anderson's head agreeing to help with the con
jokes.punchline: Her motivation is to be taken seriously as an actress despite being just a head
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor playing on celebrity vanity. A disembodied head wanting to be taken seriously as an actress is perfectly ridiculous.

jokes.text: Then he doesn't know I won the Oscar? Nope. Crap!
jokes.setup: Asking if Fry knows about her future Oscar win
jokes.punchline: Her disappointment at not being able to brag about it
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the actress vanity joke. Her frustration at not being able to show off future accomplishments is relatable and funny.

jokes.text: 10.77 same as my PIN number.
jokes.setup: Pizza and soda cost exactly what his PIN number is
jokes.punchline: The coincidence that reveals his PIN to the villains
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that drives the plot forward. The setup from earlier pays off perfectly as Fry unwittingly reveals his secret.

jokes.text: Thanks a billion. More like 4.3 billion.
jokes.setup: Pamela says 'thanks a billion' casually
jokes.punchline: Fry corrects it to his actual wealth amount
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the bank scene. Fry's literal correction of the figure shows his disconnection from normal social expressions.

jokes.text: This dream brought to you by Lightspeed Briefs.
jokes.setup: Fry waking up from another dream sequence
jokes.punchline: The underwear ad from the opening returns
jokes.explanation: Good structural callback that bookends the episode. Reinforces the dream advertising concept established at the beginning.

jokes.setup: Fry's ATM card being confiscated
jokes.punchline: The repo man knows his PIN number from the elaborate con
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that shows the con worked. The single number reveals how completely Fry was manipulated. Great economy.

jokes.text: Because I dared to dream of my own race of atomic monsters Atomic supermen with octagonal-shaped bodies that suck blood out of you
jokes.setup: Professor explaining why he's considered mad
jokes.punchline: His completely insane scientific ambitions
jokes.explanation: Perfect Professor Farnsworth character moment. The specificity of 'octagonal-shaped bodies that suck blood' is wonderfully bizarre.

jokes.text: Listen, you little bastard... I control the robot oil business... and I won't let you ruin me.
jokes.setup: Mom's sweet public persona
jokes.punchline: Drops facade to reveal crude threatening behavior
jokes.explanation: Good character payoff showing Mom's true nature emerging under pressure

jokes.text: You might as well put that checkbook away because I've discovered something even more important my friends, and they aren't worth even a penny to me.
jokes.setup: Fry rejecting Mom's money for something more valuable
jokes.punchline: Says friends aren't worth a penny in the process of declaring their importance
jokes.explanation: Good character growth moment with typical Fry verbal clumsiness. His mangled sentiment about friendship's value is endearingly awkward.

jokes.text: Holy hell! You're going to eat them? Oh! Well, just make sure you eat them all. You're a growing boy. Toodle-oo! Dumb ass.
jokes.setup: Mom horrified that Fry will eat the valuable anchovies
jokes.punchline: Switches to fake sweetness then immediately insults him when leaving
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Mom's rapid personality switches. The transition from fake maternal concern to crude insult is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: Gross! Ick! Mm. Eh, no one likes them at first... but they'll grow on you.
jokes.setup: Everyone disgusted by anchovies
jokes.punchline: Fry's optimistic prediction about acquired taste
jokes.explanation: Realistic character moment - Fry's defensive optimism about unpopular food

jokes.text: I don't know. I've had cow.
jokes.setup: Fry promising the most delicious extinct animal
jokes.punchline: Bender casually mentions having eaten cow, implying it's also extinct
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark casual reveal that cows are also extinct in the future. Bender's matter-of-fact delivery makes the implication hit harder.

jokes.text: That heavenly stench.
jokes.setup: Everyone else finds anchovies disgusting
jokes.punchline: Zoidberg loves the smell everyone else hates
jokes.explanation: Perfect Zoidberg moment. His alien appreciation for what humans find revolting is consistent with his character and provides a nice ending beat.

jokes.text: There aren't anymore, and there never will be. More!
jokes.setup: Fry explains this is the last of the anchovies
jokes.punchline: Zoidberg immediately demands more despite the impossibility
jokes.explanation: Good ending escalation. Zoidberg's immediate addiction and impossible demand creates a perfect final joke for the episode.
